text: generating root ideas from the seed set…

node.text: Per-shape dispatcher over three specialized kernels
node.detail: Host-side router keyed on (batch, n) picks among a register-blocked tiny-n kernel, a fused single-block mid-n POTRF, and a tiled DAG factorizer for the giants. Brute-force sweep panel width and matrices-per-block per grid entry offline; bake best configs into a static lookup table. Geomean ranking rewards never losing badly on any single entry.

receipts.quote: Reasoning-effort tuning: Medium can beat High … a cheap knob to sweep.
Understanding GPU bottlenecks is easy with a visualisation.

node.text: Register-blocked multi-matrix-per-block kernel for tiny n
node.detail: For batch=4096,n=32 through n=128 the matrices are launch-bound and one matrix cannot fill a warp. Assign multiple matrices per threadblock, hold the whole factor in registers/shared, do an in-register unblocked right-looking potf2, write L once — one launch for the whole batch.

node.text: Fused single-block left-looking POTRF for mid-n (256..1024)
node.detail: One matrix per threadblock; fuse potf2 + trsm + syrk into a single kernel to kill inter-step launch overhead and keep the panel resident in shared memory. cuSOLVERDx's inlinable device-side POTRF fused with cuBLASDx GEMM is the pragmatic middle path. Sweep NB in {32,64,128} per shape.

receipts.quote: CuTe layout/atom abstractions — memory & layout strategies.

node.text: Tiled right-looking DAG factorizer with SYRK + pipelined GEMM
node.detail: For n=8192..32768 the run is compute-bound and dominated by trailing updates. Right-looking tiled Cholesky under the R1–R8 dataflow rules; CUTLASS RankK (FillMode kLower) halves the FLOPs of the symmetric update; cp.async software pipelining overlaps the next tile's loads with the current MMA.

receipts.quote: software pipelining: overlap mem copying with compute to hide latency.

node.text: Stream-K persistent trailing update to kill wave quantization
node.detail: n=4096 is the awkward middle: the trailing GEMM under-fills the last wave of SMs. A fixed-grid persistent kernel with Stream-K decomposition splits the K-dimension across SMs so the final wave is full; multi-stream per-tile concurrency fills the machine when one factorization under-occupies.

receipts.quote: Pipeline-parallel schedules — 1F1B for peak-memory, Chimera/DualPipe for bi-directional overlap.

node.text: TF32 factorization with FP32 iterative refinement
node.detail: Factor in TF32 tensor-core precision for the big-n entries, then run one or two refinement sweeps against the FP32 input to pull the reconstruction residual back under the checker's property-based tolerance. Risky: the checker validates the residual against the original FP32 input.

text: expansion 1/3: deep-diving "Per-shape dispatcher over three specialized kernels"

node.text: Performance Tuning of Batched Cholesky (Abdelfattah et al.)
Autotuning numerical dense linear algebra for batched computation
cuSOLVERDx: achieving high performance (per-shape config checklist)
GPU MODE leaderboard 776 — benchmark grid & ranking rules

The literature confirms per-shape routing is how MAGMA and cuSOLVERDx already win: tuning tables per (batch, n) with crossover points near n=128 and n=2048. The 15 fixed grid entries make offline exhaustive sweep trivially affordable, and geomean ranking punishes any single fallback to the heavyweight host API.
node.detail: Sweep cost is real (15 entries × ~40 configs); crossover points may shift between the tuning GPU and the leaderboard GPU.

node.text: Static per-entry config table baked into the submission
node.detail: Since the 15 benchmark shapes are published, skip runtime heuristics entirely: sweep (kernel, NB, matrices-per-block) offline per entry, bake a 15-row lookup table keyed by exact (batch, n), and fall back to nearest-neighbor routing for anything off-grid.

node.text: Geomean-aware tuner: optimize the worst entry first
node.detail: Because ranking is geometric mean, a 2× loss on one entry costs as much as 2× losses spread anywhere. Tune greedily on whichever entry currently has the worst ratio to its roofline bound, not on total runtime.

text: expansion 2/3: deep-diving "Static per-entry config table baked into the submission"

node.text: MAGMA batched factorization — nFTB sweep 1..16, gains vanish past size 16
A guide for achieving high performance with very small matrices on GPU
cuSOLVERDx advanced examples: blocked_potrf.cu reference

Strongest lineage in the tree: the fixed public grid turns autotuning from a research problem into a build step. MAGMA's published sweeps give starting configs for every regime, so the table can be seeded analytically and refined with a short on-GPU sweep.
node.detail: Overfitting to the tuning GPU; needs a guard path for shapes not in the table.

node.text: Seed the table analytically from roofline class, refine on-GPU
node.detail: Classify each entry launch/memory/compute-bound from bytes-and-FLOPs arithmetic, seed configs from MAGMA's published sweet spots, then run a 5-minute local sweep only around the seed. Cuts sweep cost ~8× while keeping per-entry optimality.

receipts.quote: Understanding GPU bottlenecks is easy with a visualisation.

node.text: Ship a cuSOLVER fallback row for off-table shapes
node.detail: Nearest-neighbor routing can misfire between regimes; add one guard row that routes any off-grid shape to cuSOLVER's batched potrf so correctness and sanity are never at risk, at zero cost to the 15 scored entries.

text: expansion 3/3: deep-diving "Register-blocked multi-matrix-per-block kernel for tiny n"

node.text: Batched one-sided factorizations of tiny matrices using GPUs
MAGMA fixed-size batched Cholesky — already register-blocked for n≤128
torch.linalg.cholesky_ex dispatches to cusolver batched potrf under batch>1

The deep-dive undermines a hand-rolled kernel as a separate branch: MAGMA already ships exactly this design, and the dispatcher lineage (t10 → t21) subsumes it as regime-A's table entry. Standalone effort duplicates a solved problem with high implementation risk for zero marginal geomean gain.
node.detail: Keeping it would split effort across two lineages that produce the same regime-A kernel.

markdown:
# Exploration Brief — GPU MODE — batched dense Cholesky factorization (leaderboard 776)

> Distilled from a recursive idea tree seeded by your curation: 8 unread saves read, 12 signals scouted, 10 ideas explored (1 pruned). Every idea carries receipts back into your library or to discovered sources.

## 1. Static per-entry config table baked into the submission · score 9/10

*Lineage: Per-shape dispatcher over three specialized kernels → this*

Since the 15 benchmark shapes are published, skip runtime heuristics entirely: sweep (kernel, NB, matrices-per-block) offline per entry, bake a 15-row lookup table keyed by exact (batch, n), and fall back to nearest-neighbor routing for anything off-grid.

**Deep-dive verdict.** Strongest lineage in the tree: the fixed public grid turns autotuning from a research problem into a build step. MAGMA's published sweeps give starting configs for every regime, so the table can be seeded analytically and refined with a short on-GPU sweep. Risks: overfitting to the tuning GPU; needs a guard path for shapes not in the table.

**Receipts:** [https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2018/icl-utk-1337-2018.pdf] ("nb × (warps-per-block, block-dims) sweep methodology."); [doc_3a11e71404b6574d68a309d098103406] ("a cheap knob to sweep")

## 2. Per-shape dispatcher over three specialized kernels · score 9/10

Host-side router keyed on (batch, n) picks among a register-blocked tiny-n kernel, a fused single-block mid-n POTRF, and a tiled DAG factorizer for the giants. Geomean ranking rewards never losing badly on any single entry — the dispatcher's one job is to never fall back to the heavyweight host API where the shape is launch-bound.

**Deep-dive verdict.** The literature confirms per-shape routing is how MAGMA and cuSOLVERDx already win: tuning tables per (batch, n) with crossover points near n=128 and n=2048. Risks: crossover points may shift between the tuning GPU and the leaderboard GPU.

**Receipts:** [doc_3a11e71404b6574d68a309d098103406] ("Reasoning-effort tuning: Medium can beat High … a cheap knob to sweep."); [doc_ba165183b5f6f46b622bc9311ed2eee6] ("Understanding GPU bottlenecks is easy with a visualisation.")

## 3. Seed the table analytically from roofline class, refine on-GPU · score 8/10

*Lineage: Per-shape dispatcher → Static per-entry config table → this*

Classify each entry launch/memory/compute-bound from bytes-and-FLOPs arithmetic, seed configs from MAGMA's published sweet spots, then run a short local sweep only around the seed. Cuts sweep cost ~8× while keeping per-entry optimality.

**Receipts:** [doc_ba165183b5f6f46b622bc9311ed2eee6] ("Understanding GPU bottlenecks is easy with a visualisation.")

## 4. Fused single-block left-looking POTRF for mid-n (256..1024) · score 8/10

One matrix per threadblock; fuse potf2 + trsm + syrk into a single kernel to kill inter-step launch overhead and keep the panel resident in shared memory. cuSOLVERDx's inlinable device-side POTRF fused with cuBLASDx GEMM is the pragmatic middle path between hand-rolling and the heavyweight host API.

**Receipts:** [doc_44ae53055d24b1ee5ec5f7b995c7ec6e] ("CuTe layout/atom abstractions — memory & layout strategies.")

## 5. Tiled right-looking DAG factorizer with SYRK + pipelined GEMM · score 7/10

For n=8192..32768 the run is compute-bound and dominated by trailing updates. Right-looking tiled Cholesky under the R1–R8 dataflow rules; CUTLASS RankK (FillMode kLower) halves the FLOPs of the symmetric update; cp.async software pipelining overlaps the next tile's loads with the current MMA — exactly the technique in your saved Colfax tutorial <https://research.colfax-intl.com/cutlass-tutorial-design-of-a-gemm-kernel/>.

**Receipts:** [doc_5f11c747ee974d16234fa90c6b91cb9c] ("software pipelining: overlap mem copying with compute to hide latency.")

---

*Pruned en route: a standalone register-blocked tiny-n kernel (score 4) — MAGMA already ships exactly this design; the dispatcher lineage subsumes it as regime-A's table entry.*
